rulings.Case Title: PAK HING YU VS. HIN DUN KONG et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, April 1, 2014, line 2. CROSS DEFENDANT ZHONG WU'S DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT. Sustained with ten days leave to amend. As to the third cause of action for fraud, the Second Amended Cross-Complaint ("SACC") is barred by the statute of limitations. (CCP sec. 338.) Cross-Complainant is given leave to amend to allege facts, if he can in good faith, that establish delayed discovery, as argued in Cross-Complainant's opposition brief. As to the first and second causes of action for indemnification and apportionment of fault, the demurrer is sustained with ten days leave to amend because the SACC does not allege a basis for tort-liability against Cross-Defendant. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: ST. FRANCIS - ONE CRESCENT WAY CONDOMINIUM VS. TOP VISION DEVELOPMENT, LLC, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT /Adjudication;
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, April 1, 2014, line 4. CROSS DEFENDANT BAY CITY MECHANICAL, INC'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT /Adjudication. Denied. Bay City Mechanical (¿BCM¿) has not met its initial burden. BCM entered into a Subcontract with James E. Roberts-Obayashi Corp. (¿JERO¿). (UMF no. 3.) The Subcontract states ¿[JERO] General Terms and Conditions attached are hereby made part of this agreement.¿ (Decl. of Bobby Amos, Ex. A.) The Terms and Conditions state in the first sentence that ¿Copies of the General Contract¿are in the office of the Contractor and have been examined by the Subcontractor ¿Subcontractor agrees that he is familiar with all of the terms, conditions, and obligations of the general contract¿and that he enters into this Agreement based upon his investigation of all such matters and is in no way relying upon any representations of Contractor¿It is further agreed that the contract documents are incorporated in this Agreement by this reference with the same force and effect as if they were set forth herein, and that Subcontractor will be bound by any and all of said contract documents insofar as they relate in any way, to the work covered by the Agreement. Subcontractor agrees to be bound to the Contractor in the same manner and to the same extent as Contractor is bound to Owner under the contract documents.¿ (Decl. of James Orellana, Ex. 1.) The Prime Contract provides at Sec. 13.7 that ¿Notwithstanding any of the foregoing to the contrary, the statutory limitations period with respect to ¿latent deficiencies¿¿shall commence upon the later of (1) the time described at Paragraph 13.7.1 above or (2) upon the Owner¿s discovery of the Latent Deficiency and its cause.¿ (Decl. of Miloslav Khadilkar, Ex. G.) CCP sec. 337.15 is ¿an ordinary, procedural statute of limitations.¿ (Brisbane Lodging L.P. v. Webcor Builders, Inc. (2013) 216 Cal.App.4th 1249, 1258.) Sophisticated parties may contractually alter the statute of limitations. (Id. at 1263, ¿[W]here the parties are on equal footing and where there was a considerable sophisticated give and take over the terms of the contract, those parties should be given the ability to enjoy the freedom of contract and to structure risk shifting as they see fit without judicial intervention.¿) Thus, BCM and JERO agreed, at the time they entered into the subcontract, that BCM would be bound by the Prime Contract, including Sec. 13.7. BCM presents no evidence that it was not a sophisticated party at the time of contracting or that it did not have the opportunity to negotiate the subcontract. The unambiguous language of the subcontract states that BCM agreed that it was familiar with the terms of the Prime Contract at the time it entered into the subcontract. The evidence shows that the earliest the Owner could have become aware of defective construction was in 2004. (UMF no. 15.) The complaint in this action was filed on October 24, 2011, less than 10 years from the earliest date the Owner could have discovered any defects. (UMF no. 27.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party¿s counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: ELVIA JUAREZ VS. ANTHONY E NOVAK et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Edward Novak'S Motion Of "Demand Motion" And "Demand Motion" For Protective Order And For Sanctions At Misused And Abusive Discovery Devices
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Tuesday, April 1, 2014, Line 6, Defendant Edward Novak'S Motion Of "Demand Motion" And "Demand Motion" For Protective Order And For Sanctions At Misused And Abusive Discovery Devices Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant¿s motion for a protective order that ¿certain matters not be inquired into¿ prospectively, is denied. Although on very rare occasions an order limiting the scope of discovery is possible, it is rarely granted. Attorney client communications or attorney work product in general are not discoverable. However, there are exceptions. Until such time as potentially privileged communications or privileged work product are sought through a formal discovery device, there is no basis to rule on whether any of those exceptions apply. The request for sanctions is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to steve@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/jpt

rulings.Case Title: VICTOR S. BRICE VS. MATIASIC ROTH & JOHNSON LLP et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION Re The Existence Of A Fiduciary Duty And Duty To Comply With Rule 3-310 Of The Rules Of Professional Conduct And Evidence
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, April 1, 2014, line 11. PLAINTIFF VICTOR BRICE'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION Re The Existence Of A Fiduciary Duty And Duty To Comply With Rule 3-310 Of The Rules Of Professional Conduct And Evidence. Motion for summary adjudication as to three causes of action for declaratory relief, professional negligence, and breach of fiduciary duty is denied. Motion for summary adjudication on the issues of existence of a fiduciary duty and duty to comply with Rules of Professional Conduct 3-310 is denied. Each cause of action and issue of duty relies, as a threshold matter, on the existence of an attorney-client relationship. The formation of an implied-in-fact attorney-client relationship depends on the facts and circumstances of each case, and particularly on the parties' intent and conduct. (See Miller v. Metzinger (1979) 91 CA3d 31, 39; Hecht v. Sup. Ct. (Ferguson) (1987) 192 Cal.App.3d. 560, 565.) Plaintiff has not met its burden to show no triable issue of material fact as to whether the parties intended to enter such a relationship, where Plaintiff may have been represented by separate counsel by the time of Defendant's retainer by GuideOne Mutual Insurance Company. Additionally, Plaintiff has not shown that the work performed by defendant included legal advice, and was not of such a "preliminary and peripheral nature" as to avoid disqualification. (See Marriage of Zimmerman (1993) 16 CA4th 556, 5665-66.) As such, there is a triable issue of material fact as to whether an attorney-client relationship was formed to give rise to a fiduciary duty and a duty to comply with Rule 3-310 of the Rules of Professional Conduct. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: BOB PLANTHOLD VS. STATE OF CALIFORNIA et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, April 1, 2014, line 14. DEFENDANTS SENATE COMMITTEE ON TRANSPORTATION AND HOUSING, CALIFORNIA STATE SENATE, CALIFORNIA STATE ASSEMBLY, SENATOR MARK DESAULNIER, ASSEMBLYMEMBER ISADORE HALL, III, SENATE COMMITTEE ON RULES' DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. The demurrer is sustained without leave to amend as to the entire amended complaint. The Legislature has plenary authority over its internal rules. See People's Advocate, Inc. v. Sup Crt. (1986) 181 Cal.App.3d 316, 322; Ex parte D. O. McCarthy (1866) 29 Cal. 395, 403. The Legislature did not violate plaintiff's due process or freedom of speech rights by suspending its internal rules and enacting Assembly Concurrent Resolution No. 65. Procedural due process principles apply to adjudicative acts, not legislative ones. See Horn v. County of Ventura (1979) 24 Cal.3d 605. Plaintiff fails to allege how he has a property interest in the Bay Bridge or how the Legislature violated plaintiff's freedom of speech rights. rulings.Case Title: BOB PLANTHOLD VS. STATE OF CALIFORNIA et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, April 1, 2014, line 14. DEFENDANT STATE OF CALIFORNIA'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. The demurrer is sustained without leave to amend. The state is not a person under 42 USC 1983 and therefore, the first and third causes of action fail as a matter of law. See Will v. Mich. Dept. of State Police (1989) 491 U.S. 58, 64. The remaining causes of action fail because plaintiff fails to show that the State can provide plaintiff any relief. Lastly, plaintiff failed to provide any substantive opposition to the State's demurrer.
